other_observers: None; To my knowledge, I was the original finder. I learned at the scene of the Ross's Gull at Cherry Creek Res in Aurora that Dan Maynard also saw this bird (not sure of the date, but probably 11/06/2010).
specific_location: Last Chance water treatment area (the sewer pond) just west of the private Rest Area at the junction of SR71 and US36.
description: This was a very plain warbler, typical warbler size and shape, thin billed. Overall bird was gray above, pale yellowish below with indistinct stripes on the underparts, with two bold white wingbars. Strong, thick whitish-yellow supercillium which extended back onto the sides of the neck but did not appear to connect with the stripe on the other side of the head (i.e., did not form a "collar"). Wide gray stripe on the side of the crown, which gave the bird a gray-capped look (except for the pale stripe in the front of the crown with provided a "part"). Faint yellowish, central forecrown stripe. Back gray with faint tan stripes. Area below the eye marked by a broad pale yellow arc. Lores from the eye to the bill medium gray. Bill longish, somewhat downcurved, and mostly dark with apparently pale inner mandible margins. Auricular area plain gray and set off from the light arc below the eye and pale yellow supercillium. Rear of the auricular slightly darker than the main part. Throat pale yellow. Breast pale yellow with broad, faint stripes (not unlike a purple finch in the lack of sharp definition to their edges). Flanks also faintly striped. Belly whitish, apparently mostly unstriped (not seen all that well). Wings blackish with bold white wingbars, upper wingbar being slightly more conspicuous. Flight feathers appeared mostly black with white edges. Leg color dark.Behaviors: Very active, not seen all that well in any one spot, moving all around at about 15 feet high above the ground in the exotic Siberian Elms that ring the water hole. Apparently seeking European Elm Flea Weevils (a new exotic insect in CO that appears to be replacing the long-standing "default" insect of elm foliage in CO, the also-exotic Elm Leaf Beetle). Did NOT confirm the food item, however. Disappeared before I could get great looks or decent photos, unfortunately.Call: Chip note heard, typical warbler, harsh, "-ck" at end, difficult to describe in a way that would be helpfulPlumage: first-winter
